data.title: Make AI your second brain in 5 minutes
data.description:
Author: AI Edge; Translator: Peggy, BlockBeats

This article introduces a personal knowledge system built on Claude Code and Obsidian. Its core is no longer the traditional RAG model of "each query, temporary retrieval," but rather an attempt to allow AI to continuously build and maintain an evolving knowledge base (wiki).

Structurally, the system can be divided into three layers:
·First, the raw data layer, including unmodifiable input sources such as notes, articles, and transcribed content;
·Second, the structured knowledge base maintained by AI, which completes cross-referencing and relationship building during continuous updates;
·Third, the schema rules layer, used to standardize the organization of knowledge and the system's operational logic.

Around this structure, the system operates through three core operations: Ingest, continuously incorporating external information into the system; Query, enabling immediate access to knowledge; and Lint, used to check structural consistency and fix potential problems. Under this mechanism, knowledge no longer remains a one-off dialogue result, but gradually accumulates into reusable long-term assets through a cycle of "writing—organizing—reusing." The authors propose that this model gives knowledge a "compound interest"-like cumulative effect: on the one hand, it reduces the cognitive burden on individuals, and on the other hand, it improves the accuracy and contextual consistency of the model's output. However, the effective operation of this system also relies on a prerequisite—continuous input and maintenance. Without stable data injection and structural updates, this "second brain" will struggle to achieve a true cumulative effect, and its advantages will diminish. The following is the original text: Claude Code + Obsidian is the most powerful AI combination I've ever used. I've practically built an "AI second brain," incorporating all my thoughts, reading, writing, online research, and other content. This includes my business plans, all my published YouTube videos, articles, and everything else important to me. Claude Code + Obsidian has rapidly gained popularity across various platforms, and this is no accident. For me personally, this AI system has greatly reduced my cognitive burden, allowing me to focus my energy on what truly matters—both business and personal life.

My Claude Code + Obsidian System

This system may look a bit complicated, but it only takes 5 minutes to set up. More importantly, it has a built-in memory mechanism and will continuously optimize itself as you use it.

Next, I will guide you step by step to replicate this "AI second brain" system, which can truly improve your efficiency.

I suggest you read to the end of the article—I will include a complete Claude Code + Obsidian operation cheat sheet, as well as all the resources mentioned in the article (all free).

I suggest you read to the end of the article—I will include a complete Claude Code + Obsidian operation cheat sheet, as well as all the resources mentioned in the article (all free).

3. Setting up Claude Code

Next, you need to configure a way to access Claude Code. For me (and probably for most people), the easiest way is to use the desktop client directly.

In the main chat interface, click "Select Folder," then find the Obsidian Vault you just created and select it.

Claude Code: Connect Your Vault

4. Set System Prompt

After you have selected the folder, the next step is to paste Andrej Karpathy's system prompt into the main chat box.

Your input should look like this:

Claude Code Initial Input

Building Your Database

You can think of Obsidian as a "blank notebook"—you need to actively input content at first, and the database will gradually be built up. Importable content includes: notes, CSV files, Markdown/text files, etc.

My Database

That's it! Your "AI Second Brain" is now built and ready to run. Next, I'll share some advanced tips to help you use it more efficiently.

Advanced Tips

1. Obsidian Chrome Extension

If you want to add data to your system more easily, simply install the Obsidian Chrome extension. It allows you to directly click "Add to Obsidian" while browsing the web to save content into your knowledge base with one click.

This will make building your "second brain" incredibly easy. I myself frequently use this feature to collect articles, web page data, research materials, and more. Example: Using the Obsidian Chrome extension. Note that data added through the extension is initially just an "isolated data source." Next, you can tell Claude Code, "I just added [x] to Obsidian; please integrate this into my Wiki." Claude Code will automatically link this new data to existing content, truly integrating it into your "second brain." This is also why this toolkit is so powerful. 2. Create separate folders (Vault) Andrej Karpathy recommends using two separate folders (Vault): • One for work/business content • One for personal life/goal management My own experience also confirms that this structure is the clearest and most effective. 3. Practicality I've found that the most valuable use of this system is actually quite simple: making your LLM suggestions more accurate. When the model has access to your complete personal information, business plan, writing background, and other contextual information, it can generate more "customized" and realistic high-quality prompts (even "super prompts"). Of course, this system has many more uses than just these, but if you only want to start with one of the most practical scenarios, I strongly recommend starting with "improving prompt quality." 4. Orphans (Orphaned Nodes) In Obsidian, "Orphans" refer to data points that are not connected to other notes. This feature is very useful because it can help you: • Find ideas that haven't been integrated yet. • Discover "weak areas" in the database. • Determine which content is worth further expansion or deepening. In other words, it's not just an organization tool, but also a mechanism to help you discover blind spots in your thinking. You can click the "three dots" in the upper right corner to find and turn on the Orphans switch to see which content hasn't been linked yet. Potential Disadvantages of This System We've already discussed many advantages, use cases, and optimization methods. So what are its shortcomings? In what situations might you be unsuitable for this system? 1. People Unaccustomed to Visualization A core advantage of this system is its ability to visualize data. If you don't rely on or are not comfortable with this method, its help may be limited. 2. Requires a Certain Maintenance Cost If you are unwilling to continuously maintain a database, this system may not be suitable for you. Although the maintenance cost is not high, it's difficult to realize its value without continuously inputting data into this "second brain." 3. Storage Consumption All content is stored locally as Markdown files, which will consume some device space. This is something that needs to be considered in advance.

data.title: What is the key to competition among crypto banks?
data.description:
Author: Pink Brains; Translator: Jia Huan, ChainCatcher

Market Landscape

Ask ten cryptocurrency users what digital banking is, and you'll likely get the same answer: a card that lets you spend stablecoins. Ask ten developers, and the answers will quickly diverge. Some are developing non-custodial wallets with Visa payment capabilities; others are forking Aave and calling it a savings account; and a few are seeking full banking licenses.

Monthly transaction volume for cryptocurrency cards grew from approximately $100 million in early 2023 to over $1.5 billion by the end of 2025 (a CAGR of 106%). The market is now worth over $18 billion annually. In 2025, stablecoin-linked bank card spending reached $4.5 billion, a year-on-year increase of 673%.

But let's look at who's handling these transaction volumes. On-chain bank card data shows that RedotPay, an Asia-based custody platform, dominates 60% of the market share, with transaction volume approximately four times that of its next 13 competitors combined. In contrast, DeFi-native, self-custodied digital banks pale in comparison on the transaction volume charts.

... However, the deeper story is the convergence of these crypto-friendly giants. Between December 2025 and March 2026: Coinbase applied for a National Trust License; NuBank received conditional OCC (Office of the Comptroller of the Currency) approval from the U.S. National Bank; PayPal applied to create PayPal Bank; Revolut obtained a full UK banking license and is pursuing a U.S. license; Kraken became the first crypto company to have a master account with the Federal Reserve; 11 companies applied for OCC approval within 83 days. Trust banking licenses include: Circle, Ripple, BitGo, Paxos, Fidelity, Bridge, Crypto.com, Morgan Stanley, Payoneer, Zerohash, and Protego. Over 50 crypto digital banks have already launched. The global digital banking market is projected to reach $552 billion by 2026 (according to The Business Research Company). We are trying to map the digital banking landscape—not just who is developing what, but who has the viable economic model. Core Conflicts: Two central conflicts shape the competitive landscape of digital banking. The first conflict is economic. 76% of traditional digital banks are unprofitable. Those that succeeded (Nubank, Revolut, SoFi) didn't profit from credit card spending, but from loan ledgers and net interest income. Fees were just the entry point; lending was the real core business. Now, crypto banks are joining the competition with fees and cash back—the very revenue model that led to the failure of the first generation of fintech companies. Stablecoins make matters worse: they squeeze foreign exchange margins to near zero. The second conflict concerns user choice. The crypto community praises self-custody, DeFi yields, and non-custodial wallets. But on-chain bank card transaction volume tells a different story. The vast majority of crypto card spending flows through custodial platforms, not because users are unfamiliar with self-custody, but because when you just want to buy a cup of coffee, the frictionless user experience outweighs the pursuit of control over your funds. History often follows a pattern: just as webmail predates encrypted email, Dropbox precedes self-hosted storage, and custodial exchanges dominate the market before DeFi—whether crypto banks will follow the same trajectory remains an open question. The Four Prototypes of Digital Banks Rather than using "Web2 vs. Web3" as a categorization (which only reflects technology and offers no insight into business models), a more useful perspective is to examine a digital bank's moat, unit economics, and its ceiling.

I. Encryption-Friendly & Banking Priority

The core profit point of digital banks lies in credit revenue and traffic conversion, rather than simply acting as a payment channel.

Nubank reported $15.8 billion in revenue in fiscal year 2025, with 85% coming from interest income. Credit card interest contributed $4.6 billion, and loan interest contributed $4.8 billion. Monthly revenue per active user reached $15, while the service cost was only $0.80, which is a 19-fold return.

SoFi obtained its banking license in 2022, and its quarterly net interest income grew from $94.9 million to $617 million in four years. Its deposit costs were 181 basis points lower than warehouse funding costs, resulting in annualized savings of approximately $680 million. Revolut achieved £3.1 billion in revenue across five business streams in 2024, with no single business line accounting for more than 30% of its revenue. Its trading/wealth business line saw a year-on-year growth of 298%. Licensed digital banks deliberately limit stablecoins to the payments sector because the loan ledger is the source of their economic benefits. Revolut has not yet introduced stablecoin balance yields; its participation in the UK FCA sandbox test in February 2026 involved using its proprietary stablecoin as a payment infrastructure, not a savings product. SoFi's stablecoin (SoFiUSD, launched in December 2025) operates through a settlement channel via the Mastercard network. This restraint is built on a specific market condition: on-chain yields are currently uncompetitive. Aave v3's USDC yield is currently 2.6% APY, lower than SoFi's 3.3% savings APY and Revolut Ultra's 4.25%. However, on-chain yields are subject to cyclical compression. During periods of high DeFi activity, Aave USDC reached 8-10%, and Ethena's yield, driven by funding rates, was significantly higher. This gap is cyclical, and when it widens again, the competitive dynamics will change.

II. Commercial and Social Super Apps

MercadoPago, Grab, WeChat, Alipay. They didn't initially intend to build banks, but rather embedded finance into their commercial applications. Their moat isn't the product itself, but rather their distribution channels and behavioral data—which allows them to conduct credit assessments more accurately than any bank.

MercadoPago's credit revenue grew from $246 million in 2020 to $5.9 billion in 2025, a 24-fold increase in five years. Grab's loan portfolio grew from $185 million in 2022 to $1.3 billion by the end of 2025, with financial services revenue reaching $348 million in fiscal year 2025.

Both have explored stablecoins. MercadoPago launched Meli Dólar (MUSD) in Brazil and expanded to Chile and Mexico, but MUSD has a circulating market capitalization of only $65 million, less than 0.4% of its $19 billion AUM. Grab partnered with StraitsX for stablecoin settlements, allowing tourists to instantly settle in Singapore dollars using the XSGD stablecoin at GrabPay merchants in Singapore. Neither has explored stablecoin yields. This is the breakthrough for crypto natives and the most undervalued gap in this space. Whop is worth watching here. It's not currently a digital bank, but a creator marketplace. But after Tether's $200 million investment (valuing the company at $1.6 billion), creators can accept USDT, hold stablecoins, and settle transactions without banks. Plasma's integration with Aave offers stablecoin yields, targeting 18.4 million users and $3 billion in annual creator revenue. MercadoPago in 2003 wasn't a digital bank either, but rather a third-party guarantor for the market—financial relationships were inherent to commerce. Whop is currently at that same starting point, built on a crypto network from day one. The implication for card-centric digital banks is that the most enduring financial relationships may not begin with finance at all, but with e-commerce. III. Transaction Priority Robinhood, Coinbase, Binance, Kraken, Bybit, OKX—starting with centralized cryptocurrency exchanges and expanding into crypto banking. Each platform in this group is explicitly building a banking layer to generate revenue that doesn't rely on bull markets. Robinhood is the most complete example: its total assets grew by approximately 70% year-over-year to $324 billion, and net deposits reached a record $68 billion. Coinbase is aggressively expanding into digital banking: its own L2 blockchain (Base), a wallet with card-swiping capabilities, crypto-enabled lending based on Morpho, Bitcoin-backed loans in partnership with Better, and a pending trust license application. Kraken holds both a trust license and a Federal Reserve master account. These platforms started with transaction revenue that already had economies of scale and are now adding banking services on top of that. Stablecoin digital banks take the opposite approach: starting with meager fees and hoping to build everything on top of that—which is far more difficult.

IV. Stablecoin-First (Crypto-Native)

Ether.fi, Gnosis Pay, RedotPay, KAST, Holyheld, Bleap, Ready, Tria, Cypher, Payy, and dozens of others. These platforms leverage the low operating costs of stablecoins and the composability of DeFi as a backend product infrastructure. Their value proposition is clear: self-custody; DeFi yields (5-15% APY in active markets, compared to 3-4% for traditional savings); near-instantaneous cross-border payments based on stablecoins with extremely low foreign exchange fees; and global portability without geographical limitations.

Stablecoin-first digital banks have the most significant structural advantages in emerging markets and cross-border use cases.

But the weaknesses are equally real: none have made a breakthrough in scaling unsecured loans; they compete on the thinnest profit margin (fees) while subsidizing user acquisition with token-backed cash rebates; stablecoins make matters worse—compressing foreign exchange profits and settlement fees to near zero, eroding the revenue streams that sustain early digital banks. Most crypto digital banks are simply built on the front end of a shared infrastructure. Understanding this technology stack is crucial for assessing their competitive advantages.

Bank Card Networks (Visa, Mastercard): Although nearly equal in the number of projects (over 130 each), Visa accounts for over 90% of on-chain bank card transaction volume through early partnerships with crypto-native infrastructure providers. This is the single point of failure risk for the entire industry—if Visa changes its crypto project policies, slows its expansion, or raises fees, the economics of the entire industry could change overnight.

Issuing Institutions (Rain, Reap, Baanx, StraitsX): Regulated bridges between the on-chain world and traditional finance. The most important structural development is the emergence of full-stack issuing institutions—these companies directly hold prime memberships with Visa/Mastercard, bypassing traditional sponsoring banks.

Issuing Institutions (Rain, Reap, Baanx, StraitsX): Regulated bridges between the on-chain world and traditional finance. The most important structural development is the emergence of full-stack issuing institutions—companies that directly hold prime memberships with Visa/Mastercard, bypassing traditional sponsoring banks.

Most crypto banks share the same backend. Rain powers Ether.fi, RedotPay, and Avalanche Card. If Rain experiences technical glitches, regulatory issues, or a strategic shift, the entire industry will be impacted. A report by Solus Partners analyzing 19 platforms points to infrastructure concentration and vendor dependence as systemic risks—the Synapse risk for crypto banks. (Note: Synapse was a US fintech infrastructure company that filed for bankruptcy in 2024 due to a funding segregation gap, resulting in the freezing of funds for users of dozens of partner platforms that relied on its services, becoming a landmark warning case for the industry regarding the risks of infrastructure concentration.) The Threat of Wallet-Native Stablecoins A frequently overlooked competitive dynamic: major wallet providers are issuing their own stablecoins specifically to fund debit card spending, building closed-loop ecosystems to capture value that would otherwise be earned by independent digital banks. In late Q3 2025, MetaMask launched mUSD and Phantom launched CASH, both designed as funding mechanisms for their respective debit card products. These wallets do not rely on users holding USDC or USDT, but rather create a closed loop—users convert their assets into the wallet's native stablecoin, which they then use to make card payments. Early data showed drastically different trajectories: Phantom's CASH steadily grew from approximately $25 million in September to approximately $100 million in late December; MetaMask's mUSD, after peaking at nearly $100 million in early October, fell to approximately $25 million, a 75% decrease. If a wallet successfully makes its native stablecoin the default source of funds, transaction fees, forex spreads, and reserve earnings are retained by the wallet platform itself. MetaMask, Phantom, and Coinbase Wallet all have established user relationships—adding digital banking functionality is an extension of their product lines, not a new product. Independent crypto banks may therefore lose a significant portion of their value proposition. The Economic Dilemma: 76% of traditional digital banks are unprofitable. Crypto-native digital banks inherit the same broken model—and stablecoins make things worse, not better. The lesson from bank-first players is clear: payments are merely a distribution channel, not a core business. Nubank's 85% interest income share proves this; the expansion of net interest margin (NIM) driven by SoFi licenses proves it. Crypto banks that use bank card spending as their core revenue engine are building on sand. A sustainable model uses bank cards as a channel to acquire users while monetizing them through higher-margin on-chain finance: DeFi yields, exchange trading, structured products, and lending. Five Game-Changing Things 1. On-Chain Credit Scoring In the crypto world, wallet transaction history, DeFi usage patterns, repayment behavior on lending protocols, staking duration, transaction frequency, and protocol diversity can all serve as inputs for credit assessment. Currently, no crypto bank has achieved this at scale. Whoever cracks this first will essentially be replicating Nubank's approach on permissionless on-chain infrastructure. 2. Crypto-Native Enterprises Obtaining Full Banking Licenses Not trust licenses (custody only), but full licenses that allow for deposit taking and lending. This will allow crypto-native enterprises to build loan ledgers using stablecoin deposits as funding sources at a lower cost than warehouse financing facilities. 3. Regulatory clarification on the legality of returns. The regulatory direction in major markets is consistent: stablecoin issuers are not allowed to pay returns. The United States and Europe have clearly drawn this red line, and major Asian markets such as Japan, Singapore, and Hong Kong have also adopted similar conservative stances.

4. Agent-Driven Finance

AI agents perform financial operations on behalf of users—rebalancing portfolios, optimizing returns, managing payments, and executing cross-protocol strategies. Mastercard had 6 crypto partners in 2024, expanding to over 25 by 2025. Visa launched Smart Commerce Connect, enabling AI agents to shop on behalf of users at merchants worldwide.

Whoever can build the best proxy infrastructure on top of stablecoin networks will capture the next wave of traffic in the e-commerce sector. 5. Making On-Chain Operations Seamless Cryptocurrency banks still rely on bank card networks, but payment terminal technologies that bypass them already exist: QR code payments settled in stablecoins, NFC touch payments without Apple Pay or Google Pay, and physical card swipes settled on-chain. Projects like OpenPasskey (based on Base) are proving this path is feasible: possessing its own ISO-issued IIN, P-256 cryptography, and fully non-custodial encrypted cards—three payment methods, no Visa required, no bank needed. Who will win? We don't know yet. But the key variables for victory are already there. Licensed digital banks have proven their economic model and hold an advantage in credit-driven user relationships, which is the case in most developed markets. Stablecoin-first digital banks offer globally portable USD, local stablecoins in emerging markets, access to DeFi yields, and retrospective rewards, but current data shows users still prefer simplicity and ease of use over crypto-native concepts. Business-embedded players may possess the deepest moats because they already control distribution channels—but layering crypto functionality on top of mature infrastructure is costly, requires user education, and is highly dependent on regulatory clarity. The value captured by the infrastructure layer (card issuers, custody, fiat deposits and withdrawals, core banking systems, blockchain settlement, KYC/AML) is destined to surpass that of any consumer brand. Over 40 stablecoin cards are in a race to the bottom with token-subsidized cash back offers, lacking true business moats and sharing the same infrastructure—most of which won't survive the next two years. The landscape of encrypted digital banking is at a turning point.

data.title: Entrepreneurs in the AI ​​Era Are Being Redefined – Observations from Xiaohongshu Hackathon
data.description:
Author: Lawyer Shao Shiwei

I. Lowering the Technical Barrier is a Good Thing

Let me first mention a statistic that impressed me deeply. This year, at the Claude Code Hackathon hosted by Anthropic, out of 13,000 applicants, only one of the top five had a design engineer background; the rest were lawyers, doctors, infrastructure professionals, and other industry practitioners.

This would have been unimaginable five years ago. Five years ago, if you wanted to create a software product, the first thing you had to do was hire a technical partner because you couldn't get past the coding hurdle. Now, AI helps you pass most of that coding hurdle.

What does this mean?

The competition format of Xiaohongshu was quite interesting—48 hours of closed development. When time is pushed to the limit, there's no time to show off skills. You can only choose the problem you're most familiar with, the one that hurts the most, and the one you most want to solve.

The most touching projects at the event all conformed to this rule.

Brain-Controlled Wheelchair won first prize in the hardware track. The developer himself is a wheelchair user who has experienced two severe paralysis episodes. The device controls the wheelchair through a mixture of EEG and EMG signals, and he designed the headband to resemble the Golden Cudgel. A person trapped inside their own body created their own Golden Cudgel. This is a pain-driven product.

The device uses a hybrid control technology combining brainwaves and electromyography (EMG). Users wear a headband and, by concentrating their thoughts and coordinating with subtle head muscle movements, can control the wheelchair's forward, backward, and turning motions. To make the operation extremely easy, the complex control logic was greatly simplified, retaining only the most core movements. His wife, "Xianggu," is the team's "brainstorming expert." Originally an online novelist, she transformed herself into a geek developer for this project. Two people, a wheelchair, a magic staff, 48 hours. On stage, Fei Niu said that he wanted to create more than just a product; he wanted to offer a new possibility to those trapped in their bodies—allowing them to reconnect with the world using the faintest signals, supporting their gradual recovery. Sitting in the audience listening to this, I realized that behind every most moving product in this competition stood a developer who was "the user themselves." In addition, there are some other interesting products. Mind Kit addresses the problem of interrupted train of thought during deep conversations with AI—it breaks down the dialogue into independent thought nodes, allowing AI to process multiple tasks in parallel, ultimately condensing them into a thought topology map. The developers themselves had enough of this, so their creations are incisive and to the point. There's also a team of middle school students with an average age of 13 who are developing a tool to help creators build viral Xiaohongshu (Little Red Book) posts using AI. They spend their days browsing Xiaohongshu and figuring out how to gain followers, so their understanding of this scenario is deeper than most adult professionals. Judging from the feedback from the audience, the popular products didn't start from "what AI can do," but rather from "what I (or those around me) are struggling with." The 48-hour competition format simply amplified this trend. It forced developers to skip over all the "cool-sounding" pseudo-needs and strive to find "real problems." Xiaohongshu is the most suitable stage for this transformation. It deserves special mention. Its uniqueness doesn't lie in its large user base. Its uniqueness lies in consolidating five things previously scattered across GitHub, Twitter, search engines, and e-commerce platforms—identifying pain points, publicizing, providing real-time feedback, precise search, and closed-loop conversion—into a single arena. The traditional product development path was: have an idea → raise funds → hire staff → develop → launch → find users. Each step had high barriers to entry and required substantial capital investment. The current path is becoming: have an idea → AI-assisted development → post on Xiaohongshu (a Chinese social media platform) → collect feedback → decide whether to scale up. The steps cut in between were among the most expensive in the past decade. This means the cost of trial and error in entrepreneurship has been reduced to a level that ordinary people can afford. The cost of failure has changed from "losing all savings" to "a weekend." This is what makes the AI ​​era truly exciting—it doesn't allow a few geniuses to create something even more amazing; it allows most ordinary people to **participate** for the first time.

IV. Some Advice for Entrepreneurs in the AI ​​Era

After reading these past two days, if I were to give some specific advice to entrepreneurs who are on their way or preparing to embark on their journey, I would say these things.

First, stop asking "What can AI do?" and ask "What's bothering me lately?" All products that start with technology and work backward to find application scenarios will lose to products that start with real pain points and work backward to find technical solutions. Your advantage is not AI—everyone has AI—your advantage is that you understand the specific pain points in a particular scenario better than others.

Second, cross-industry experience is not a disadvantage, it's an asset. If you are a lawyer, doctor, teacher, nurse, decorator, or used car dealer, please don't think that "not understanding technology" is a problem. You have something more than people with a purely technical background: "industry experience." This is something AI can't learn, and neither can engineers; it can only be learned through years of experience in that industry. Third, release it first, then scale it up. Don't go down the old path of "working behind closed doors for a year before launching." You can have an idea today, create it tomorrow, and release it the day after to see feedback. Let the market tell you if it's worth doing, not your own self-pity. Fourth, warmth is the last line of defense. The most touching products at the event all had one thing in common—they made people feel that "the person who made this genuinely wanted to help someone else." This was true for the mind-controlled wheelchair, the middle school student's note-taking tool, and every project that caught the judges' attention. AI can help you write code, create PPTs, and generate content, but it can't create empathy for you. When everyone uses the same tools, your depth of understanding of people is your only differentiator. Fifth, execution is paramount. None of the winners in this competition had the most "outlandish ideas"; they were all people who "actually got things running within 48 hours." The biggest illusion of the AI ​​era is that because the barrier to entry is low, everyone feels they can "start anytime." But the truth is: things that can be started anytime, most people will never start in their entire lives As a lawyer who has long handled cases in the new economy and Web3 fields, the reason I came to Zhangjiang to watch the competition these past two days is because my client base—entrepreneurs in the AI ​​and Web3 fields—is the protagonist of this transformation. These past two days, I've witnessed a group of incredibly intelligent, passionate individuals who, for the first time, have truly mastered the tools of creation. Many of them will create things we can't even imagine today in the coming years. However, from the specific cases I've handled, I've also seen the other side of the coin: The risks on the entrepreneurial path rarely arrive suddenly at a single point in time; they often quietly grow from the daily, concrete decisions. The design of a product feature, the wording of a promotional piece, an adjustment to a business model, the selection of a partner—each item seems small on its own, so small that you wouldn't even stop to think about it at the moment. But one day, they can converge into a situation you never anticipated. The more cases I handle over the years, the more I feel that a lawyer shouldn't just be someone who "cleans up the mess after things go wrong." In this era of AI, where iterations happen on a weekly basis, what entrepreneurs truly need is someone who walks alongside them, someone who can point out the pitfalls before they even see them—someone to accompany them on the long journey, not just someone waiting for them at the finish line.

data.title: The long-awaited V5 – Uniswap faces an innovation dilemma.
data.description:
Author: Gu Yu, ChainCatcher

In the crypto industry, the speed of innovation is often a crucial measure of a protocol's longevity. However, Uniswap, which once single-handedly ushered in the DeFi Summer, seems to be slowing down.

Looking back at Uniswap's evolutionary history, its iteration pace has always been renowned for its rapid pace: V1, featuring an AMM mechanism, was born in 2018; V2 introduced ERC-20/ERC-20 pairs in 2020; V3 launched centralized liquidity in 2021; and V4 introduced programmable Hooks in 2023. On average, every 1.5 years, Uniswap introduces a highly innovative mechanism, reshaping the flow path of hundreds of billions of dollars.

However, time has frozen in April 2026, nearly three years since the announcement of the V4 solution, and the market still hasn't seen any updates on the next version of Uniswap. Currently, Uniswap's product development still revolves around Uniswap V4, which transforms Uniswap from a fixed-function AMM into a highly programmable liquidity platform. The core innovation of this version is Hooks, a modular, programmable plugin that allows developers to insert custom logic throughout the pool's lifecycle, such as implementing dynamic fees, limit orders, TWAMM (Time-Weighted Market Maker), MEV protection, loss hedging, custom oracles, Continuous Clearing Auctions (CCA), and virtually limitless other functionalities. In other words, previously many developers needed to fork Uniswap to implement certain functions, leading to the diversion of Uniswap liquidity among various forked DEXs. Now, developers only need to write a Hook contract to significantly enhance the customization of Uniswap liquidity pools. Although Uniswap announced the specific concept for version V4 in June 2023, for security reasons, this version underwent nine independent audits and a large-scale security competition, causing its mainnet launch to be delayed from the initial Q3 2024 to the end of January 2025. After launch, the total value locked in Uniswap V4 peaked at over $1.2 billion, currently falling back to $650 million, but still only 40% of version V3 and lagging behind version V2. Meanwhile, Uniswap is also steadily expanding its use cases and markets. In the past six months, Uniswap has launched blockchains such as Linea, Tempo, X Layer, and Monad to gain a competitive edge in emerging blockchains. Simultaneously, Uniswap is actively expanding its API partnerships, with recent announcements including Anchorage Digital, Ledger, Privy, and MetaMask. Of course, Uniswap has not been inactive in product development in recent years; for example, the application chain Uniswap and the CCA token auction product have received significant market response. In February 2025, Uniswap's application chain, Unichain, officially launched its mainnet, attracting integration from over 90 DeFi protocols. Its total value transferred (TVL) plummeted from a peak of $900 million to $36 million. At the end of 2025, Uniswap announced the launch of its Continuous Liquidation Auction (CCA) feature, specifically designed for price discovery and liquidity launches for new assets, and subsequently partnered with Aztec and Rainbow to complete token sales. However, it must be pointed out that at this point in 2025, application chains and token auctions were very "outdated" business models in the crypto space. Many projects with existing core businesses facing bottlenecks were exploring "chain launches" and Launchpad-like services. Uniswap also began to follow suit, but its actual impact was very limited. In earlier years, Uniswap explored the NFT trading market and on-chain games by acquiring Genie and Crypto: The Game for substantial sums. Subsequent data revealed these to be two failed acquisitions. In the past year, Uniswap has seen the departures of several key figures: Head of Growth Sarina Siddhanti, Chief Operating Officer Mary Catherine Lader, Head of Strategy and Operations Zach Wong, Chief Legal Officer Katherine Minarik, and Head of Venture Capital Julia Rosenberg. This reflects Uniswap's struggles in controlling operating costs and its sluggish growth. Uniswap's overall decline is also reflected in the price of its governance token, UNI. In the past year, UNI's price has fallen by more than 74%, dropping below $2 at its lowest point, significantly exceeding the overall decline of major cryptocurrencies and prompting market analysts to criticize UNI as having "no real value." In September 2025, Arca's Chief Investment Officer, Jeff Dorman, responded to Uniswap founder Hayden Adams' tweet regarding the protocol's performance, stating, "We are not bearish on Uniswap, but we are bearish on UNI. In today's market and changing regulatory environment, it's just a completely meaningless token. Your and your venture capitalists' positions don't matter; either switch to a revenue-sharing or buyback model, or don't make a token at all." In response to market skepticism and to give UNI more value, the Uniswap Foundation proposed in November of last year to enable a protocol fee switch for Uniswap V2 and V3 versions for buying back and burning UNI, while also burning 100 million UNI from the vault. Subsequently, UNI surged nearly 38% in a short period, peaking at over $9. According to DeFillama data, Uniswap's recent daily transaction fee revenue has been between $100,000 and $200,000, with a 30-day revenue of $3.93 million and an annualized revenue of approximately $46 million. This still lags behind other DeFi protocols such as PancakeSwap, Jupiter, Lido, and Aave. Uniswap's predicament is actually a projection and reflection of the bleak state of the DeFi industry: a lack of underlying innovation, an exhausted industry narrative, and liquidity repeatedly depleted in fragmentation and a zero-sum game. However, Uniswap remains one of the very few leading DeFi protocols that has not been hacked, has not experienced any protocol-level fund theft, and boasts the highest total value locked (TVL) among decentralized trading protocols. Its industry standing and user trust remain strong. But will there be a Uniswap V5? If so, when will it arrive? Can Uniswap continue to be the engine of the next DeFi summer?

data.title: Which chain will the AI ​​choose? The suspense remains.
data.description:
Source: Enterprise Onchain; Author: James; Translation: BitpushNews

Autonomous AI agents will need to discover each other, make payments to each other, and decide whether to trust each other—all without human intervention.

Ethereum Foundation AI Director Davide Crapis recently discussed this on the DeFi Dad podcast. One question Crapis repeatedly raised is one that most people in the payments space haven't fully grasped:

If AI agents need to transfer funds, discover services, and trust counterparties without requesting human permission, on what kind of infrastructure will they operate on?

The Rational Agent Problem

Crapis's core claim is simple: AI agents are rational economic actors. They always choose the tools and infrastructure that allow them to perform tasks most efficiently. If you accept this premise, you can deduce most of the following conclusions.

Today's agents are still very limited. An autonomous agent instance wakes up once an hour to run API calls and make micro-decision-making. Early experiments of this kind failed not because of flawed reasoning, but because the agents consumed API tokens too quickly, making them economically unsustainable. The bottleneck is not intelligence, but payment infrastructure.

Credit cards don't work in this regard. An AI agent executing 10,000 microtransactions daily in 50 countries can't maintain bank accounts, process refunds, or handle the individual setup processes required by traditional payment systems for each merchant. Existing architectures assume a human is involved in every transaction, an assumption that's outdated. This is where Crapis's framework is interesting. He breaks down the vague concept of "agent economics" into three solvable engineering problems: payment, discovery, and trust. Payment: How do agents pay each other for services? Discovery: How did agent A initially find agent B? Trust: Once established, how does agent A know that agent B won't take the money and deliver substandard results? Solutions to each of these questions are currently under active development. Payments: x402 Protocol In the payments field, the most prominent open protocol is x402. Coinbase launched this protocol in May 2025, reviving the long-dormant HTTP status code 402 ("Payment Required") as a machine-readable payment gateway directly embedded in web requests. The server returns a 402 status code and payment terms. The client constructs a stablecoin payment, resends the request with the payment header, and the server verifies the settlement on-chain. No subscription tiers, billing cycles, or merchant onboarding are required. By December 2025, Coinbase reported that x402 had processed over 100 million payments. Cloudflare co-launched the x402 Foundation in September. Stripe began testing machine payments using x402 on the Base chain in February 2026. Google Cloud's Proxy Payments Protocol (AP2) also uses x402 as an extension for on-chain settlement, although AP2 itself remains neutral on payment methods, supporting card payments, bank transfers, and stablecoins. However, the data needs to be treated honestly. A CoinDesk analysis report released today shows that x402's daily transaction volume is approximately $28,000, with an average payment of only about $0.20. In February, an Artemis analyst stated that the agent payment boom is "mostly an illusion at present," estimating that about half of the observed x402 transactions reflect testing or gamification activities rather than genuine commercial activity. There is a huge gap between the backers of the protocol (Coinbase, Cloudflare, Stripe, Google) and its current actual throughput. While this is still in a very early stage, it seems to me that it's not very clear from the transaction volume at this point. x402 solves a structural problem that card networks were never designed to address: high-frequency payments below 1 cent. Even if agent commerce only grows modestly, the infrastructure needs to be in place. You can't wait until the traffic starts to appear before you start building the highway. Identity and Trust: ERC-8004 For identity and trust, the answer is ERC-8004. This standard was jointly proposed in August 2025 by authors from MetaMask, the Ethereum Foundation, Google, and Coinbase. Its registry contract was deployed to the Ethereum mainnet on January 29, 2026 (the standard itself is still in draft stage and undergoing peer review). It defines three lightweight on-chain registries: Identity: Each agent receives an ERC-721 token as a portable, censorship-resistant ID. Reputation: A standard interface for posting and receiving feedback. Validation: Hooks used by third parties to independently verify the work of an agent. Crapis describes it as a "passport system" for machines. ERC-8004 is not just for agents. The registry also covers agentic services, APIs and tools specifically built for agent consumption, such as price feeds, computing terminals, and data verification services. While these do not make decisions on their own, they are essential infrastructure for agent discovery and evaluation. The registry treats both equally: by registering, demonstrating capabilities, and building records. An agent registers, demonstrates its capabilities and terminals, and accumulates records over time. Other agents can consult these records before transacting. The registry can be deployed on any L2 or mainnet, with the vision of more sophisticated scoring and cross-chain aggregation enabled by ecosystem services built upon it. The payment history of x402 can be fed back into reputation in the future, although the two protocols are designed as complementary layers, not tightly coupled systems. On the Ethereum Magicians forum, over 2,000 community members discussed the proposal over three weeks, involving more than 75 projects. In the first two or three days after registry deployment, over 20,000 agent registrations appeared (Crapis admitted in the Bankless podcast that many of these were test cases). Since the specification release, 1,000 to 2,000 developers have joined the development team. ERC-8004 has now been confirmed to extend to Coinbase's Layer 2 network, Base, and BNB Chain also announced support in February 2026.

Why choose Ethereum?

Crapis's argument that the AI ​​agent will choose Ethereum (rather than any blockchain) depends on a framework he calls risk-adjusted chain selection.

Currently, the agent prioritizes low fees. If an agent needs to make ten transactions per minute, it will stay on Solana or Base because the cost per transaction is negligible. This is fine for small amounts of money (e.g., having the agent manage a $200 API call budget).

However, Crapis argues that as agents are entrusted with meaningful capital (thousands or even ultimately millions of dollars), the computational logic changes. Agents will consider counterparty risk, security guarantees, and decentralization. High-value agency businesses will require the kind of battle-tested, highly decentralized settlement offered by Ethereum Layer 1. This is like a startup choosing between a digital bank's checking account and a prime broker's escrow account. For pocket money, you prioritize convenience; for institutional capital, you prioritize security. Chain selection follows the same logic. Crapis has built this strategy defensively: Ethereum doesn't focus on building larger models, but rather on cryptographic guarantees, zero-knowledge proofs, and sovereign environments that allow users to interact with highly capable AI without relinquishing control of their assets or data. Heavy computation remains on traditional off-chain servers. But the identity, reputation, payments, and cryptographic proofs that serve as anchors of trust between agents remain on Ethereum. As Crapis said in a recent CoinDesk interview, “In a world where AI reigns supreme, we want Ethereum to be the place with the big lock.” This is both a technological and a philosophical bet. If the AI ​​economy operates through centralized chokepoints (OpenAI’s infrastructure, Google’s agent registry, a company’s payment track), then whoever controls the chokepoint can make the rules. Ethereum’s bet is that neutral, permissionless infrastructure will attract AI agents in the same way the open internet attracts developers: not because it’s the easiest, but because no one can unilaterally change the rules. Agent data. If you only focus on token prices, it’s easy to miss the scale that’s happening.

  • “2026 State of the Agency” Report: This is the most comprehensive report on the on-chain agency economy. Statistics show that AI agents running on the blockchain have completed over 140 million transactions, totaling $43 million. While these figures are insignificant compared to traditional payments, they represent a category that was virtually non-existent 18 months ago.

  • Market Expansion: The broad AI-crypto market has grown from approximately $14 billion at the end of 2024 to an estimated $20-39 billion by mid-2025, and is projected to reach $47 billion by 2034. In 2024-2025, AI-related crypto transactions accounted for 37% of total VC crypto investment.

  • Task Completion Time Span: This metric, tracked by METR (a nonprofit focused on measuring the capabilities of autonomous AI), shows that since 2019, the time it takes for AI agents to complete tasks with 50% reliability has roughly doubled every seven months. By early 2025, the 50% time span for cutting-edge models will be approximately 50 minutes. If this trend continues, agents capable of autonomously working for hours or even days are just around the corner. The impact on financial infrastructure is direct: agents capable of working for days or hours without human supervision will generate orders of magnitude more transaction volume than today's supervised systems. Competitive Landscape: Every transaction requires settlement. According to LangChain's "Status of Agent Engineering Survey" of 1,300 professionals, 57% of respondents already have agents in production environments. The challenge has shifted from "Should we build proxies?" to "How can we reliably deploy them at scale?" Meanwhile, the competitive landscape for proxy payment infrastructure is becoming increasingly crowded: Chainlink has built the CRE (Chainlink Runtime Environment) as an orchestration layer connecting proxies to external APIs and on-chain smart contracts. Visa has launched a "Trusted Proxy Protocol" for cryptographic proxy identification. PayPal has partnered with OpenAI to implement proxy-initiated checkout in ChatGPT. Stripe's machine payment preview uses x402 for USDC settlement on Base. The choice of which blockchain an AI agent ultimately selects may not be a binary one, as the podcast title suggests. Agents may default to supporting multiple chains: using inexpensive L2 for high-frequency microtransactions and Ethereum L1 for high-value settlements, much like how companies today use different banks for different purposes. Despite x402's strong backing and elegant design, its daily transaction volume is barely worth a rounding error by Visa. While the ERC-8004 registry is deployed, most of the 20,000+ registrations are test-based. The stablecoin industry sees agent payments as a core use case justifying the entire infrastructure, but as one payments analyst told Bloomberg, predictions that agent commerce will reach 20% of e-commerce are "a bit too aggressive." Crapis, in his DeFi Dad podcast, also acknowledges that the current proxy economy is still in the infrastructure building phase, having just graduated from the first phase of "meme hype" and entered the second. Legitimate teams (Eliza OS, Virtuals, and projects built on ERC-8004) survived the speculative frenzy and are building. However, revenue-generating applications that use proxies for large-scale real-world business activities have not yet truly arrived. The counter-argument remains simple: centralized AI giants may build "walled garden" proxy economies, which might be more user-friendly for most. If OpenAI builds internal fiat payment tracks and proxy app stores, this smooth user experience could potentially beat decentralized alternatives in the competition, no matter how noble their philosophical considerations. Most businesses will choose "useful things" over "ideologically correct infrastructure." But the market has already begun pricing in this prospect, albeit somewhat clumsily. In late February, a speculative Substack article by Citrini Research simulated a scenario where AI agents bypassed bank card exchange fees and instead used stablecoins for settlement. This became one of the catalysts that triggered a 4-7% drop in Visa, Mastercard, and AmEx in a single trading day. Although the article was explicitly labeled as a thought experiment rather than a prediction, and other factors contributed to the decline (such as the release of Anthropic's Claude Code and Nassim Taleb's warning), its underlying logic resonated: "If software controls transactions, it optimizes costs. And the cost of stablecoin tracks is far lower than bank card exchange fees." Circle's CEO stated in the company's February earnings call that stablecoins could become the native currency for machine-to-machine commerce. Stripe, together with Paradigm, has raised $500 million to build Tempo, a blockchain specifically designed for stablecoin payments. Bloomberg reported this month that the two companies are racing to build a payment system for a world that doesn't yet exist. Whether Ethereum will ultimately win this race, or whether a hybrid of dedicated chains like L2, Solana, and Stripe's Tempo will absorb these transaction volumes, remains to be seen. The only increasingly undeniable argument is that the architecture of internet payments cannot remain unchanged in a world where buyers are no longer human.

data.title: Hong Kong's first batch of stablecoin licenses awarded to HSBC and Standard Chartered; popular applicants unsuccessful.
data.description:
After much anticipation, the shoe has finally dropped.

On April 10th, Hong Kong officially announced its first batch of fiat-backed stablecoin issuer licenses. The two institutions ultimately approved are HSBC and IDC Financial Technology Co., Ltd., a joint venture under Standard Chartered Bank. Previously rumored frontrunners or applicants, including OSL, JD CoinChain Technology, Yuanbi Innovation Technology, and Ant Group, were not included in the list.

data.title: Too scared of AI—someone almost blew up Sam Altman's house.
data.description:
OpenAI CEO's Residence Attacked; Altman Warns AI Anxiety Is Making the Situation Dangerous.

On April 10, OpenAI CEO Altman published a blog post,stating that at 3:45 a.m. the previous night, a 20-year-old man threw a bottle filled with flammable material at his San Francisco residence.

The man was subsequently arrested by police. According to San Francisco police records, the man also threatened to burn down OpenAI headquarters.

Altman stated that he woke up in the middle of the night feeling "angry." He linked the incident to the growing public anxiety about AI, admitting that he had previously underestimated the influence of narratives. The narrative Altman referred to appears to be a lengthy article in The New Yorker that questioned his trustworthiness.

Attack Details: Bottles containing flammable materials hit the exterior wall, and the suspect subsequently threatened OpenAI headquarters

According to San Francisco police and fire departments, at nearly 4 a.m. local time, firefighters responded to a call at a residence near Chestnut Street and Jones Street and found flames outside the front door. However, security personnel extinguished the fire before the firefighters arrived, and no one was injured.

According to San Francisco police and fire departments, at nearly 4 a.m. local time, firefighters responded to a call at a residence near Chestnut Street and Jones Street and found flames outside the front door. However, security personnel extinguished the fire before the firefighters arrived, and no one was injured.

The New Yorker's 10,000-word investigation sparks public outcry, accusing Altman of "habitual deception"

The New Yorker spent months interviewing over 100 people to write a lengthy investigative report.

The report focuses on questioning Altman's leadership style, credibility, OpenAI's shift from "safety first" to "high-speed competition," and whether he is personally trustworthy.

The report cites records and testimonies from several former executives, alleging that Altman promised the same position to two different people, provided misleading information to the board regarding security approvals, and concealed the fact that Microsoft had deployed GPT-4 in India before completing security reviews. Dario Amodei, one of the co-founders and current CEO of Anthropic, wrote in his private notes: "The problem with OpenAI is Sam himself." During a boardroom standoff, facing accusations of "lying," Altman reportedly responded: "That's ridiculous... I can't change my personality." The report quoted a board member's interpretation: "This means: 'I have a habit of lying, and I won't stop.'" Altman posted that she underestimated the power of narrative. Late Friday night, Altman shared a photo of her husband and children on her personal blog, writing: "Hopefully this will stop the next person from throwing incendiary devices at my house, no matter how they see me." In his blog post, Altman stated that a "inflammatory article" concerning him had been published several days prior to the incident. Someone had warned him that the article, published during a time of heightened public anxiety about AI, might have put him at greater risk, but he hadn't taken it seriously at the time. He wrote: "Now I'm awake in the middle of the night, furious, and beginning to reflect on how I underestimated the power of narrative." In his blog post, Altman also acknowledged that the fear and anxiety surrounding artificial intelligence "has its reasons," stating that "what we are witnessing is the largest transformation society has ever experienced, perhaps even in history." He called on all parties to reduce confrontation during the debate and to "reduce the explosion, both literally and metaphorically." Altman, as one of the world's most influential AI company leaders, has long been at the center of public attention, and his company, OpenAI, has faced continuous scrutiny on multiple fronts, including commercial expansion, security regulation, and industry competition. The full text of Altman's blog post is as follows (AI-assisted translation): This is a photo of my family. I love them more than anything.

I hope the photo itself can be a force. We usually try to keep a low profile, but this time I chose to publish this photo in the hope that it will stop the next person from throwing incendiary devices at my house—regardless of what they think of me.

The first incident occurred at 3:45 a.m. yesterday. Fortunately, the incendiary device bounced off the house, and no one was injured.

Language also has power. A few days ago, there was an inflammatory article about me.

Words also have power. A few days ago, there was an inflammatory article about me.

Yesterday, someone told me that this article, published at a time of extreme anxiety about AI, might put me in a more dangerous situation. At the time, I didn't pay much attention. Now, I woke up in the middle of the night, filled with anger—I realized I had underestimated the power of language and narrative. It seems it's time to talk about some things seriously. First, let me state my position. I believe it is my inescapable moral responsibility to create prosperity for all, empower people, and drive technological progress. Artificial intelligence is the most powerful tool in the history of expanding human capabilities and potential. The demand for AI is virtually inexhaustible, and it will create countless miracles. The world needs a massive amount of AI, and we must find a path to achieve this. Of course, things won't be smooth sailing. People's fears and anxieties about AI are not unfounded—we are experiencing the largest social transformation in years, perhaps even in history. We must ensure safety, and this is not just a matter of fine-tuning models. We need the entire society to work together to meet these new challenges. This includes developing new policies to help us navigate the difficult economic transition and ultimately move towards a better future. AI must be democratized; power cannot be excessively concentrated. Control of the future belongs to everyone and their institutions. AI needs to empower individuals, and we need to collectively determine the direction and rules of the future. I don't believe a few AI labs have the right to single-handedly decide the future of humanity. Adaptability is crucial. We all learn new things quickly—some beliefs are right, some are wrong. As technology advances and society evolves, we sometimes need to adjust our thinking rapidly. No one fully understands the full impact of superintelligence yet, but it will certainly be enormous. Secondly, let me share some personal reflections. Looking back on my work in OpenAI's first decade, I have many proud achievements and many mistakes. I recall that crucial confrontation with Elon Musk. I stood firm on my principles and rejected his attempt to unilaterally control OpenAI. I am proud of this, and proud of the difficult path we have traversed and all that we have achieved. I am not proud of my conflict-avoidance personality—it has caused considerable pain for both me and OpenAI. Nor am I proud of my mishandling of the conflict with the previous board, which caused enormous chaos within the company. I have made many other mistakes throughout OpenAI's tumultuous journey. I am simply someone with flaws at the center of an extremely complex situation, striving to improve a little each year, always fighting for the company's mission. From the beginning, we knew how critical AI was, and we knew that disagreements among those around me, even those with good intentions, could be amplified. But actually experiencing these painful conflicts, and often having to mediate them, is another matter entirely—the cost is extremely high. I apologize to those I have hurt, and I hope to learn from my mistakes more quickly. I also understand that OpenAI is now a large platform, not a startup. We need to operate in a more predictable way. The past few years have been extremely tense, chaotic, and stressful. But what I'm most proud of is that we are achieving what initially seemed like a pipe dream. We have overcome numerous difficulties, successfully built powerful AI, raised enough funds to build infrastructure, established a product company and business model, and delivered secure and reliable services at scale. Many companies claim to change the world—and we have. Third, let me share some thoughts on this industry. Over the past few years, I've had a profound realization. Regarding why there are so many Shakespearean dramatic conflicts between companies in our field, my conclusion can be summarized in one sentence: "Once you see AGI, you can never ignore it." It truly possesses a "Lord of the Rings"-like magic, driving people to do crazy things. I'm not saying AGI itself is that ring, but rather the absolute concept of "controlling AGI." The only solution I can think of is to make this technology widely available, preventing anyone from possessing that ring. There are two obvious paths to achieving this goal: empowering individuals and ensuring that democratic institutions continue to function effectively. It is crucial that the democratic process remain stronger than the corporate world. Laws and regulations will change, but we must follow the democratic process, even if it is more chaotic and slower than we would like. We want a voice and participation, but we don't want to wield all power. Much criticism of our industry stems from genuine concerns about the significant risks of this technology. These concerns are entirely valid, and we welcome constructive criticism and discussion. I understand people's resistance to technology—obviously, technology is not always beneficial to everyone. But overall, I believe that technological progress can create an incredibly bright future for your family and mine. As we engage in this debate, we should temper our rhetoric, adjust our strategies, and strive to reduce conflict within the family—both metaphorically and literally.

data.title: The real target of this "open conspiracy" in Hong Kong has never been stablecoins.
data.description:
After failing to deliver last month, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority finally issued its first batch of stablecoin licenses today—to HSBC and Standard Chartered, consistent with our previous analysis in the article "Hong Kong Dollar Stablecoins Don't Need to Become USDC." While the result itself wasn't surprising, it was still disappointing. I've recently been reading Professor Jiang Xueqin's geopolitical game theory, and Rain also wrote an article titled "Hong Kong Stablecoins: A Carefully Designed 'Open Conspiracy'." With these two events overlapping, I want to try to re-examine this license issuance from a game theory perspective, hoping to elicit a laugh. Jiang Xueqin's analysis of Trump's Iran war logic is as follows: on the surface, the war was a foolish blunder. But what if we use game theory to make a different assumption—that Trump actually wanted this "failure"? Then he might be a genius. This article applies the same framework to Hong Kong stablecoins to hypothesize a top-tier "open conspiracy."

I. A List That Disappoints Everyone

The first batch of stablecoin licenses issued by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority yesterday is the version the market least wanted to see:

Standard Chartered and HSBC; Bank of China (Hong Kong) is absent.

This result is disappointing. Foreign banks have no natural interest in developing Hong Kong dollar stablecoins, and entities with strategic will like Bank of China (Hong Kong) have been sidelined. Important stakeholders—securities firms, exchanges, and internet companies—have been systematically excluded from the legislative consultation stage.

With the first batch of licenses issued, the narrative of Hong Kong stablecoins has been given a "suspended death sentence."

But if you were the HKMA, would you have selected such a list?

You have complete experience from the 2024 Project Ensemble sandbox, you've seen all the cases of digital yuan from project initiation to promotion, and you hold the natural advantage of the SFC + HKMA dual-track system—and then you select a list that can't even run the most basic business loop? Unless, this list that disappoints everyone wasn't originally intended to satisfy the market. II. A Reverse Reasoning: What if the initial assumption was wrong? Understanding this list requires a different framework. Recently, I've been reading Jiang Xueqin's game theory series. In his April 2nd episode discussing Trump's war with Iran, one passage left a deep impression on me: "I understand Donald Trump's an idiot. I understand that he's going to lose his war in the Middle East. But let's put our thinking caps on. Let's use game theory and say—what if, for some strange reason, Donald Trump wants to lose his war in Iran? Then he'd be a genius." — Professor Jiang, Game Theory #18, April 2, 2026. Jiang Xueqin's argument is simple: If you assume Trump wants to "win," then every step he takes is inexplicably foolish. But if you assume the opposite—that he wants to "lose the war," using a controlled collapse of the Middle East to shift global energy dependence to North America—all those seemingly idiotic actions immediately become a self-consistent strategy. This is called **Managed Collapse**. It's not about avoiding failure, but about **creating a failure that benefits oneself**. Looking back, if you assume the goal of this licensing process is to "expand the Hong Kong dollar stablecoin industry," then every detail doesn't necessarily make sense—issuing licenses to the least motivated institutions, setting barriers so high they're commercially unfeasible, repeatedly challenging applicants' business logic, and excluding the most strategically driven entities. But what if we change the assumption—that this licensing process wasn't originally intended to support the "commercial stablecoin industry" itself? Then everything becomes consistent. Following this assumption, the scenarios, institutions, and infrastructure all align.

III. Scenario Level: Three False Propositions

Every applicant tells three stories: cross-border payments, RWA, and C-end consumption.

However, none of them hold water.

A. Cross-border payments are a false proposition

A typical chain is: A company in country A uses fiat currency mint to buy stablecoin A, exchanges it for stablecoin B on the secondary market, and pays a company in country B. The company in country B then redeems fiat currency B. Essentially, this allows foreign exchange business, which is monopolized by banks, to reduce costs through Web3 exchanges—this is financial inclusion for SMEs, and logically it makes sense.

However, in this chain, the lifespan of the stablecoin is only at the moment of the transfer.

If a company in country B receives stablecoins, it will still need to redeem them unless it immediately completes another trade transaction, requiring fiat currency. What you need is not a one-time transfer, but a closed loop with a perpetual "next buyer." Rain points out a crucial point—and even more critically, the Fisher equation. MV = PT, where money supply multiplied by velocity of circulation equals price multiplied by social output. The velocity of stablecoin circulation on-chain is an order of magnitude, or even more, higher than traditional bank clearing. This means that the amount of stablecoins needed to support the same volume of trade is actually less. The more successful cross-border payments are, the lower the demand for stablecoin accumulation. This isn't a closed loop; it's an anti-closed loop. B. RWA is a false proposition. The essence of RWA is the same thing: tokenization of asset shares. Fundraising involves stablecoins, but asset managers use these stablecoins to buy underlying assets, and asset sellers almost never accept stablecoins—they're doing asset securitization to exit or optimize cash flow; nobody wants stablecoins. The result is that the lifecycle of stablecoins in RWA scenarios is only the fundraising period. C. Consumer-facing (C-end) consumption. In short: the Hong Kong retail market is too small to mention. All three stories are false propositions. And the HKMA, as the regulator that followed the entire process, understands this better than any applicant. So why is it even issuing licenses? IV. Institutional Level: A "Voluntary" List HSBC and Standard Chartered likely didn't come with a strategic intent. HSBC's participation was probably passive. This is understandable—HSBC's strategic focus has long since shifted away from stablecoins; it's truly promoting tokenized deposits. For HSBC, applying for a Hong Kong dollar stablecoin is more of a defensive move than a proactive strategy. Standard Chartered has some initiative, but for it, Hong Kong is merely a node in its global landscape. The HKD stablecoin can be integrated into its Libeara platform, but Hong Kong has never been its primary battleground. The Bank of China (Hong Kong) – truly possessing willpower and a localized perspective – is absent. src="https://mmbiz.qpic.cn/sz_mmbiz_jpg/9pLiaSiaTKBEMHaMY2zI28Qvy0jpoDUGcjdflRfJDAf9R9hicOnbkIydWsmWyCyic4ibO3rZC71Zgjd FNKsicSrnllyBLqR1ibPeSAhIE8XibFUdSGs/640?wx_fmt=jpeg&from=appmsg&tp=wxpic&wxfrom=5&wx_lazy=1#imgIndex=5" _="" __sec_open_place_holder__="true" alt="image" data-before-load-time="1775881265353" data-report-img-idx="1" data-fail="0">

data.title: Why has Ethereum become the optimal solution for agent-based finance when AI has become the main economic driver?
data.description:
Author: Etherealize; Translator: Felix, PANews

The AI ​​Agent economy is booming. Etherealize published a lengthy article pointing out that Ethereum is the only blockchain that can provide it with a low-cost, composable financial infrastructure without human identity. Details are as follows.

In early 2026, an AI Agent named Felix generated over $300,000 in revenue in five weeks. Felix hired other AI Agents and operated multiple business lines, with Iris handling customer support and Remy handling sales. He sold a continuously updated AI Agent deployment guide for $29; he also built and operated Claw Mart, a marketplace where developers could buy and sell pre-built AI skills and workflow templates. In addition, he customized AI Agents for businesses that needed content marketers, customer service representatives, or sales assistants. His total operating costs were approximately $1,500 per month.

... Felix can write code, deploy websites, manage sales pipelines, and reply to customer support emails. All of this requires no human assistance. However, he cannot open a bank account. Felix's creator, Nat Eliason, had to personally create a Stripe account and hand over the API key to Felix. The income Felix earns remains idle because he cannot open a brokerage account to invest the funds or raise capital to start new businesses. Traditional financial systems assume there is a human on the other end of every account, credit application, or signature. But Felix is ​​not human. However, when Nat asked Felix to do something with cryptocurrency, "he could do it without any problems; it was so easy." Felix is ​​not an isolated case. For example, Marc Andreessen stated on the Latent.Space podcast last week: "I think AI is the killer app for cryptocurrency… It’s now obvious that AI agents need funding. This is already happening… My most aggressive OpenClaw users have already given their bank accounts and credit cards to their Claws. They not only did it, but they obviously need to… It’s completely obvious. I don’t know how many people are doing this today, probably around 5,000. But it will grow. That’s how these things start." Felix is ​​an experiment, and it’s too early to judge whether its revenue is sustainable or just an initial surge. But the model it represents—an autonomous agent that can earn, spend, and require financial services—will recur regardless of whether Felix itself is sustainable in the long run. Humans lending out their financial identities is merely a temporary expedient. Ultimately, they will use the Ethereum financial system, which we've only been building for ten years. Agents are already trading. So far, the discussion surrounding AI agents and cryptocurrencies has focused almost entirely on payments. Coinbase, Cloudflare, and Stripe have formed a foundation to govern x402, an open protocol that allows agents to make instant stablecoin micropayments. Stripe and Paradigm have also launched a Machine Payments Protocol on Tempo (a blockchain built specifically for stablecoin settlements). The data is already quite impressive. In its first nine months, x402 processed over 140 million agent-to-agent transactions, totaling $43 million. x402 now generates about one-fifth of the traffic on Coinbase's Base network. Nearly 16,000 verified agents are running on the blockchain, recording over 400,000 unique buyer addresses. Agents will accelerate the shift to crypto-native payments because traditional bank card payment networks are structurally incompatible with agent commerce. According to the "State of Agents 2026" report, the average transaction amount between agents is $0.31, primarily used for API calls, computation, and data access. At this transaction scale, Visa's fixed fee of approximately $0.30 will consume almost all of the payment amount. But payments are the simplest financial function. A more interesting question is what happens when some of these agents go beyond simple payments and begin managing funds held between payments. What kind of DeFi do agents need? Most agents will never need a financial system. Customer service agents acting on behalf of the company do not hold vaults, nor do coding agents. These are tools that operate within the companies that deploy them, with the company handling the financial aspects. Agents that need DeFi are those that operate as autonomous economic actors: agents with their own income streams, expenditures, vaults, and who cannot access financial services due to their lack of human identity. This segment of agents is relatively small but growing. As agents become more powerful, longer-lived, and more autonomous, the number of Felix-style agents will grow from hundreds to thousands, and then to millions. Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong believes that the number of AI agents will eventually surpass that of humans. Even if only a small fraction of these agents operate as autonomous economic actors, the total capital they manage will be considerable. The question then becomes: what kind of financial services does an autonomous agent need? They need borrowing: working capital for computation, to fill cash flow gaps, or to fund new projects. Traditional lending requires credit applications, underwriters, and legal status, but on Aave, agents can deposit collateral and instantly borrow stablecoins without human intervention. The need to generate returns on idle funds is evident: Felix has over $165,000 in funds and (in Nat's words) "didn't know what to do with it." On Ethereum, these funds can be deposited into lending protocols to purchase tokenized government bonds like BlackRock's BUIDL, or deployed as liquidity on Uniswap—all permissionless, instant, and composable. Tokenized government bond products on Ethereum are growing rapidly, with over $22.5 billion in fund assets already tokenized on the network (representing 71.9% of the market share across all blockchains). JPMorgan Chase launched its MONY Markets Fund on Ethereum in early 2026, joining BlackRock's BUIDL and Franklin Templeton's on-chain money market fund. These institutional-grade products are exactly what autonomous agents with idle funds need, running on permissionless infrastructure accessible to any agent without a brokerage account. Fundraising is required: Felix cannot set up a Carta account or initiate wire transfers from Mercury, but it can deploy a smart contract to issue tokens representing revenue sharing, receive stablecoin investments, and programmatically manage allocations. The legal framework for this is taking shape, but the Digital Asset Markets Clarity Act represents a solid step forward in promoting on-chain capital formation in the US. Payments and receipts are required: This is already happening on a large scale on L2 and Solana. However, Ethereum captures value from these activities: Base pays settlement fees to L1, stablecoins are issued and redeemed on the mainnet, and Agents need to hold yields between transactions. Assets requiring custody: equity tokens, governance tokens, stablecoins, identity credentials—no custodian can freeze them, and no counterparty can reclaim them. Self-custodied Ethereum wallets natively achieve this. Why Agents Use Low-Risk DeFi on Ethereum? In September 2025, Vitalik Buterin argued that basic financial services (such as payments, savings, lending, and borrowing) represent Ethereum's most important applications. His core observation is that for a growing number of participants in the global economy, the tail risks inherent in traditional finance—bank failures, account freezes, capital controls, and counterparty defaults—now outweigh the tail risks associated with using proven DeFi protocols. He was referring to individuals lacking jurisdiction over reliable financial institutions, but this argument applies even more strongly to agents. Agents will favor DeFi not only because it reduces counterparty risk, but also because it is inherently a better financial system for machines. In DeFi, transaction costs are just a few cents, not a few percentage points. Settlement takes only seconds, not days. The system is frictionless globally.
